metadata.title: Matter Waves
metadata.description_blurb: Matter waves: what the de Broglie relation is, when to use it, and what it cannot tell you. The Davisson-Germer experiment, the Tonomura single-electron buildup, and a runnable simulation exercise.

beats.narration_text: The de Broglie relation assigns a wavelength to any moving object. Here is what it predicts, where it has been confirmed, and where it stops being useful.

props.command: What is the de Broglie wavelength, when does it matter, and when is it too small to measure?
output: one at a time, still makes fringes: Tonomura, 1989

beats.narration_text: In 1924, a doctoral student handed Paris a thesis so strange the committee nearly rejected it. They asked Einstein. Einstein called it brilliant.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of de Broglie (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.narration_text: His name was Louis de Broglie. The method: if light carries momentum and behaves like particles, then particles carry wavelengths. Apply the formula; test the output.

Einstein had shown light carries momentum — waves behaving like particles. De Broglie reversed it: anything with momentum has a wavelength. Decision point: this applies only at scales where the wavelength is comparable to the structure being probed.

viz.data: left: 'light waves -> particles' with a navy check (Einstein); a terracotta hand-drawn arrow flips the relation: 'particles -> waves?'; the question mark lands last

beats.narration_text: Formula: wavelength equals h divided by momentum. Use it when: the wavelength is on the order of the spacing you are probing. Do not use it when: the object is macroscopic — the wave is there, but unmeasurably small.

tangent.claim: Everything that moves has a wavelength — everything.

glossary.mean: Planck's constant — same h, third film

example.cost: Heavier things: waves too small for any instrument.

tangent.reentry: And a crystal is exactly where it was tested.

beats.narration_text: Lambda: the matter wavelength. P: momentum — mass times velocity. H: Planck's constant, 6.626 times ten to the minus thirty-four joule-seconds. Plug in the particle's mass and speed; the formula returns a length.

An electron at 54 volts: wavelength 0.167 nanometers. Nickel crystal spacing: 0.091 nanometers. Bragg's law predicts a diffraction peak at 50 degrees. Observed peak: 50 degrees. Parameter-free prediction. That is the test.
In New Jersey, Clinton Davisson and Lester Germer were firing electrons at nickel, recording boring, featureless scatter. Then an accident contaminated their sample.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Davisson & Germer (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.narration_text: Cleaning the nickel with heat turned it into a single crystal — orderly rows of atoms. The boring scatter vanished. Sharp peaks appeared at specific angles.

viz.data: left: jumbled dots (polycrystal) with a flat smooth scatter curve; heat chip; right: ordered atom grid, the curve redraws with a sharp navy peak

beats.narration_text: Electrons at 54 volts should diffract like a wave of point one six seven nanometers. Run it through Bragg's law: the peak lands where observed. Exactly.

viz.data: calc card: 54 V -> λ = 0.167 nm -> Bragg (d = 0.091 nm) -> predicted ≈ observed 50°; 'parameter-free' chip

beats.narration_text: Decades later in Japan, Akira Tonomura made it undeniable. He fired electrons through a splitter one at a time — never two in the machine at once.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Tonomura (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.narration_text: Each electron lands as a single dot. Ten dots: random. Two hundred: faint clumps. Six thousand: stripes forming. Seventy thousand: perfect interference fringes.

viz.data: THE visual: four detector panels labeled 10 / 200 / 6,000 / 70,000 — dots accumulate from random to fringed

beats.narration_text: The electrons never touched each other. Each one's wave explored both paths at once and interfered with itself. The dot is where the wave collapsed. No classical model accounts for this result.

viz.data: one terracotta dot enters; its path splits into two ghost navy waves around the biprism wire; they recombine into fringes; one dot lands

beats.narration_text: So why don't you diffract through a doorway? Compute it: seventy kilograms walking equals a wavelength of ten to the minus thirty-five meters.

shot.note: AI-generated plate — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.narration_text: That is twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton. Failure condition: wave effects exist at all scales, but become unmeasurable when the wavelength is orders of magnitude smaller than the smallest available probe.

viz.data: log scale bar: electron 0.167 nm · buckyball 2.5 pm · proton 10⁻¹⁵ m · you 10⁻³⁵ m; the last tick lands far off to the right

beats.narration_text: The frontier keeps moving: sixty-atom carbon balls diffracted in 1999. Molecules of two thousand atoms by 2019. The wave nature never stops. We just stop seeing it.

shot.note: AI-generated plate — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.narration_text: Who was this doctoral student? French aristocracy, a history degree, a Nobel Prize five years after the thesis they almost refused. A story of its own.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of de Broglie (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.narration_text: Here is what this looks like in a real session. Ask Claude to compute de Broglie wavelengths for a range of particles and voltages, then identify which cases are experimentally accessible. Then stress-test it by changing the probe to a neutron instead of an electron.

props.title: de Broglie wavelengths — runnable
props.code:
$ claude "Compute the de Broglie wavelength for: electron at 54 V, electron at 150 V, proton at 1 keV, C60 buckyball at 100 m/s, and a 70 kg person walking at 1.4 m/s. For each, state whether the wavelength is measurable given current instruments (best resolution ~1 pm). Return a table."
▸ | Particle | Voltage/speed | λ (nm) | Measurable? |
  |----------|--------------|--------|-------------|
  | e⁻       | 54 V         | 0.167  | Yes — crystal diffraction |
  | e⁻       | 150 V        | 0.100  | Yes — crystal diffraction |
  | proton   | 1 keV        | 0.029  | Yes — just |
  | C60      | 100 m/s      | ~2.5e-3 | Yes (2019) |
  | person   | 1.4 m/s      | ~1e-35  | No — 20 orders below proton |

cli_exercise.ask: claude "Compute the de Broglie wavelength for: electron at 54 V, electron at 150 V, proton at 1 keV, C60 buckyball at 100 m/s, and a 70 kg person at 1.4 m/s. For each, state whether the wavelength is measurable given current instruments (best resolution ~1 pm). Return a table."
cli_exercise.output_description: A five-row table: particle, voltage or speed, de Broglie wavelength in nm, and measurability verdict against 1 pm instrument limit.
cli_exercise.change: Replace the electron with a neutron and ask whether neutron diffraction is used in materials science today.
cli_exercise.output2_description: Table updated for neutron; Claude confirms neutron diffraction is a standard materials technique (ILL, SNS facilities), with the wavelength and use cases.
cli_exercise.next_step: Run it on a particle or molecule from your own research and check whether a diffraction measurement is feasible.

beats.narration_text: Apply the method with Humanitarians AI. More at humanitarians.ai.
